content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Sonny Mojonnier 
Founder & CEO 
WICP 
P. 0. Box 221531 
Sacramento, CA 95822 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-142 
Dear Ms. Mojonnier: 
September I , 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law; our advice is 
limited to the Act's provisions. 
QUESTION 
Docs the Act prohibit a lobbyist, whether she is a fonner legislator or not, from serving on 
the board of directors of your non-profit? 
CONCLUSION 
Based on the facts that you have provided, the Act does not limit a lobbyist's participation 
on your board of directors, as discussed below. 
FACTS 
You are the founder and CEO of Women in California Politics, a 50l(c)(3) non-profit 
organization with a mission of preserving the history of women elected to serve in California's 
legislature. To accomplish this goal, the non-profit will raise tax-deductible donations to videotape 
oral histories of these women. No current elected officials will serve on the Board of Directors. 
Some former legislators are now lobbyists and may have a role as a Board Officer or Director, as 
well as lobbyists who have not previously been public officials. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9 JO 14. All statutory 
references are to the Go\·emment Code, unless othenvise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless othenvise indicated. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-15-142 
Page No. 2 
The Act governs public officials and those who participate in the political process in 
California. Non-profits that are 501(c)(3) corporations are not political entities and must limit their 
political activity per the Internal Revenue Code. While you will be interviewing former legislators, 
you will not be making contributions or expenditures under the Act. 
Generally speaking, your non-profit will not be subject to the Act under the facts you have 
provided. Specifically, you have no elected officials as board members or decision-makers, you do 
not plan to make contributions, and the videos you make will not contain express advocacy. If any 
of these situations changes, our advice would change. Potential issues of which to be aware are: 
I. Behested payments. 
The Act states that, generally, a "contribution" includes a payment made at the behest of a 
candidate. (Section 82015(b ).) The term "candidate" includes those who hold an clected office. 
(Section 82007.) A payment is made "at the behest" of a candidate whenever it is made "under the 
control or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the 
request or suggestion of, or with the express, prior consent of' the elected officer. (Section 82015; 
Regulation 18215.3(a).) If any currently-sitting public official requests that some other entity makes 
a donation to your non-profit, that official could have reporting obligations. 
2. Lobbyists 
You stated that there may be some lobbyists on the board of directors. The Act does not 
prohibit lobbyists from serving on a non-profit board. Even so, the lobbying rules and regulations 
continue to apply to the lobbyists. For example, the$ 10 gift limit would apply as well as the ban on 
making contributions to an official the lobbyist is registered to lobby. (Sections 86203 and 85702.) 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HMR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
#J}-JJ/µ
V-
By: 
Heather M. Rowan 
Counsel, Legal Division
